storm.summary_layperson: Showers and embedded thunderstorms are in the area but tracking northeast — away from the cove — leaving heavy rain accumulation as the main hazard; a Flood Watch stays active through early Tuesday morning.
storm.local_effect_blurb: The Sound water (69°F) is slightly cooler than the air (66°F) but the gap is too small to create a protective marine layer, so the cove won't see meaningfully different weather than inland towns today.
storm.detail_html:
A cluster of radar cells sits 21–29 miles north-northwest and northeast of the cove, with maximum reflectivity of 43 dBZ — below the severe-convection threshold (45 dBZ) but strong enough to produce heavy rain and embedded lightning. The broader storm system is tracking ENE at 17 kt, carrying the cells away from Greenwich rather than toward it; every tracked cell in the arrival engine is either moving away or quasi-stationary relative to the cove.

A Flood Watch is in effect through 6 AM EDT Tuesday, issued by NWS Upton. The hazard is heavy rain accumulation from repeated rounds of showers and embedded thunderstorms — not severe supercell convection. CAPE is only 10 J/kg, which means the atmosphere is providing almost no energy for storm intensification; the 0–6 km bulk shear of 37 kt would be dangerous in a high-CAPE environment, but with virtually no instability to tap it is simply the steering current moving the cells east.

The Long Island Sound's local modifying effect is minimal today. Water temperature is 69°F versus an air temperature of 66°F — the air is actually slightly warmer than the water, so there is no marine stable layer to choke or deflect incoming storms. Storm approach is from the north and northeast rather than the southwest-to-northwest arc that most engages the Sound's deflection geometry; cells from the north move overland toward the cove with no fetch-related attenuation.

The most likely outcome for the cove is repeated rounds of heavy rain through the evening, with thunder possible during embedded cells. Because the storm motion is ENE, each round will track through quickly rather than stall. Surrounding inland towns — Stamford, White Plains — face the same risk with no local reason for the cove to see worse or better weather.

What would change the call: new convective initiation to the southwest (SW–W bearing) while the Flood Watch is active would put the approach geometry in a more concerning position. Also watch for any NWS upgrade from Watch to Warning — that would trigger a high risk-level reclassification regardless of current radar geometry.

wind.tactics_blurb: Consistent synoptic NNE flow with no thermal component — the breeze holds through sunset without collapse, softening just 1–2 kt after dark. The ongoing backing trend (ENE this morning, NNE by evening) favors a left-side start on the first beat to bank the shift; expect short oscillations around embedded showery passages.
wind.tactics_detail_html:
Today is a clean Regime B-moderate day — gradient-driven ENE/NE flow with no thermal or sea-breeze component. Both regime gates fail: coastal southerly fraction is 0% and the air-water temperature delta is inverted (–3°F, air warmer than water). The authoritative-station mean is 8 kt; the cove itself (RYC) is currently reading 9.3 kt gusting 15.4 kt, and HRRR's extended outlook for the 17–22 EDT window projects a mean of 14 kt with a range of 10–19 kt.

The direction-correction block confirms no maturation correction applies — this isn't a sea-breeze day, so the raw HRRR direction is the forecast baseline. The hourly HRRR shows a consistent left-shift (backing) through the day: from ENE (58°) at 11 AM through NE (42°) at 4 PM. Extrapolating at roughly 2–3°/hr through the evening gives NNE (39→24°) across the 17–22 racing window. At 15 kt peak around 20:00, the fleet will feel a real breeze with meaningful pressure differences across the course.

For the 17–22 racing window, expect 12–15 kt from NNE, backing from roughly 39° at 17:00 to 24° by 22:00. Gusts of 17–19 kt are possible during embedded showery passages; the max in the extended HRRR reaches 19 kt, suggesting brief stronger puffs. Post-sunset (after 20:24), the wind softens 1–2 kt as boundary-layer mixing eases — but because this is synoptically driven, there is no thermal collapse and direction holds steady.

Cove geometry for NNE flow: with the breeze from the upper-right quadrant of the compass, the left side of the upwind leg has more open northeast fetch and will see pressure first as the breeze backs. The right side picks up some CT-shore shelter. A left-start mid-line, then working right on the beat once the NNE direction firms, maximizes options across both phases of the oscillation. Avoid pinning to the right early on days with a persistent backing trend.

Tactical caveat: the AFD flags multiple rounds of heavy rain and embedded thunderstorms through tonight, with a Flood Watch active. The wind is raceable at 12–15 kt, but lightning risk from embedded cells is real — the race committee may shorten or cancel. If racing does go ahead, each showery passage will deliver a brief puff from a slightly backed direction followed by a lull, creating tactical moments to push left on headers.

data_caveats: Buoy 44040 (Western LIS) offline — UConn ERDDAP unreachable; over-water wind data absent.
HRRR evening hours (17:00–22:00 EDT) drawn from fh7–fh12 aggregate stats only — no per-hour gust breakdown available; individual-hour numbers are estimates within the 10–19 kt model range.
